title: The Macro Minute:  Will President Trump’s policy pivot sustain the bull run?

Happy Wednesday out there team42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Wednesday,
April 30th, 2025. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro question is, will
President Trump's policy pivot sustain
the bull run? Our answers are evidence
continues to mount in support of our
thesis that the bond market broke
President Trump on April 9th. President
Trump's first 100 days speech verbally
doubled down on tariffs even as he
continued to backtrack on them by
providing more delays and loopholes. He
openly also openly criticized the Fed
chair uh but he stopped well short of
calling into question Fed independence.
Although it is not our base case
scenario, risk assets can continue to
rally indefinitely if the Trump
administration and Congress pull forth
the timing of entering quote unquote
paradigm C. Uh the policy interventions
required to facilitate facilitate an
accelerated transition to paradigm C
would allow investors to look through
the U-shaped economy that remains ahead
of us by pricing in a V-shaped market
instead of the W-shaped market that we
currently still anticipate. Refer to our
April 2023rd and April 2024th leadoff
morning notes for more details regarding
paradigm C and the policies uh that uh
the Trump administration and Congress
could likely use to pull forward uh the
timing of of that outcome. The following
wisdom from our April 10th leadoff
morning note is worth reiterating today.
42 microclients, remember what we have
consistently coached you on all year.
Number one, bare markets are at least
twice as hard to risk manage as bull
markets are easy. Number two, leave the
shorting of assets to the highly paid
professionals that are being compensated
for taking on foresighted portfolio
construction risk and the added stress
that that entails. And number three, the
distribution of probable economic
outcomes is as wide as most investors
have ever seen. So remain dispassionate
and ready to pivot if if and when KISS
and Dr. Mo pivot. Odds are your odds are
you'll thank them later even if you do
not understand or trust why in the
moment the market is smarter than we are
and someone always knows something.

always wrap up with a question from our
community. Uh this one's titled uh Trump
tariffs treasury buyback style liquidity
surge equity bitcoin rally ahead. Uh
says Darius longtime sub firsttime
writer thanks for all your great work uh
chances of this happening in your
opinion. Thanks for the kind of comment.
Appreciate you guys for uh being a uh
for a long time subscriber. Um says if
Trump policies are uh trigger bond
market volatility i.e. the move spikes
and the treasury initiates buybacks um
then the dollar liquidity would rise and
hedge funds would exploit reduce margin
pressure to amplify leverage in the
basis trade. Uh the mechanism here is
the trigger tariffs create by market
stress raising volatility and margin
requirements for hedge funds. Uh the
policy is treasury ridebacks ease
liquidity strains by allowing funds to
roll out roll positions in a cheaper
more liquid bonds and lowering margin
needs. Uh and then the uh outcome is
freed up collateral enables funds to uh
increase leverage indirectly expanding
dollar liquidity despite technically
supply neutral uh buybacks. Uh this
process remembers the 2022 RRP drain uh
which preceded Bitcoin's rally and
signals uh similar liquidity driven
gains for risk assets. So uh I the
thought process here uh I like and
respect very detailed and very u uh uh
sort of u datadriven detail oriented. Uh
the problem here that I see and why I
don't think it's a high probability is
that uh one the treasury buyback
programs are like raindrops in an ocean
in terms of their size relative to the
size of the market critical treasury
market which is just shy of $30
trillion. We're talking like they're
doing like tens and hundreds of millions
of dollars of
buybacks in a marketable treasury market
that's like $30 trillion big. So, um
it's not a big enough it's not going to
move the needle uh at all. Um so what
happened in 2022 is that uh we started
to concentrate we not we treasury
secretary yellen started to concentrate
uh debt issuance incrementally on the
short end of the treasury curve to uh
for a variety of reasons uh most notably
to u satisfy robust uh you know
historically elevated market demand for
short duration instruments. um you know
we saw the reverse Fed's reverse repo
facility balloon to about$ two and a
half trillion dollars uh by you know
kind of Q3 of 2022 uh and they so she
started to essentially say well why
would I you know cause more problems in
the bond market and issue coupons and
bonds when I can just issue a bunch of
bills because there's two and a half
trillion dollars of free money sitting
there waiting to waiting for a home and
you know it's parked on the Fed's
balance sheet because it can't find a
home uh in the bill market or it doesn't
want a home in in the commercial paper
market or other short duration um credit
instruments. Uh so that was smart
policy. Uh another reason in my opinion
that was smart policy is that we saw if
you look at the spread between the
Treasury's weighted average interest
rate across the various segments of the
Treasury market, bills, notes, uh um
sorry, bills, notes, bonds, and and um
and and tips. Uh the only place in the
market where Janet Yellen was getting a
discount to issue relative to the Fed's
relative to the Treasury's existing
portfolio of debt was the bill market.
If they started dumping a bunch of bonds
and again this is back from 20 the
second half of 2022 and the throughout
2023 if they dumped a bunch of bonds on
the market what would happen is they
would have to pay a higher cost of
capital for the bonds in their portfolio
or in their in their portfolio of
leverage um because the bond yields were
much higher than the bonds uh uh yields
uh um for the existing debt. Same with
uh coupons, same with notes, uh same
with um same with tips. the only segment
of the Treasury market where there was
an actual discount in terms of market
rates being lower than the rates that
were already on the books uh were was
the bill market at that time as well. So
uh it was u you know it was a smart
policy in my opinion. I think uh the
current Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant
uh who uh he's been he was he was
hyperritical of that policy uh you know
back when she was doing it in 2023.
uh but uh since then he has come out and
reaffirmed the policy twice now in the
Q1 and Q Q2 uh quarterly refunding
announcements. So
um I think I know what we're doing here
when we talk about these uh very
esoteric uh you know liquidity dynamics.
Uh you know I think the uh the current
US Treasury Secretary made a a pivot
towards our view that we've held uh
since the uh second half of 2022. So um
there's that. Uh but getting back into
the question, um you know, I I don't
think that treasury buybacks are going
to create enough dollar liquidity to,
you know, cause a significant rally in
in Bitcoin and and and and stocks here.
Don't forget, we still have um you know,
the RP balance, which is 2002.5 trillion
back then. You know, it's somewhere
around hundred billionish dollars right
now. You just don't have enough dry
powder from that perspective to catalyze
this. Now, you could still see a wave of
liquidity come from both um you know uh
you could see a wave of liquidity come
from, you know, foreign central banks.
The Fed is eventually going to get
involved if growth slows down uh fast
enough. Um you know, again, that that's
going to be a lag uh relative probably
to other uh international central banks
like the PBOC and other uh central banks
where inflation is much less of an
issue. Um but that's that's an avenue
where we could see liquidity come from
from a global liquidity perspective. Um
and you know you you also have the
credit cycles are in an upturn as well
both domestically and globally. So uh
that's where a lot of liquidity comes
from as well as long as we continue to
see uh credit being credit expansion the
credit impulse both domestically and
globally be positive. Uh and they u they
are accelerating on a trend basis as
well. So that's noteworthy in the
context of supporting liquidity. uh but
what's uh a drag what is and what will
be a drag on liquidity uh after you know
the you know maybe after April uh is a
more than a very high probability
deceleration in global trade flows uh
and that's going to slow the
accumulation of foreign foreign exchange
reserves uh and and and and the dollar
recycling that we tend to see. So um all
told there's a you know very mixed
picture as it relates to liquidity. I
still don't see the same, you know, kind
of, you know, dramatic bull run in
liquidity that I think other analysts
have been calling for. Uh, but, you
know, I could I could see a scenario
where liquidity is higher over the
medium term and that would be supportive
uh for risk assets uh like stocks and
crypto and and Bitcoin. Uh, so just
want, you know, I'm not saying that is
the outcome, but I do believe that those
are the kinds of dynamics that would
contribute to uh kind of the underlying,
you know, question here uh uh becoming
